_doc: Aurelius benchmark baseline capability matrix. Per-baseline feature audit for every benchmark in the rollup. Read docs/BENCHMARK_BASELINE_AUDIT.md and docs/AURELIUS_PUBLIC_TRACE_BENCHMARK_ROLLUP.md before using these numbers in outreach. Simulator / public-trace results only — directional only, NOT production savings (docs/RESULTS.md §8).

audit_methodology.step_1: trace baseline implementation code (NOT doc claims)
audit_methodology.step_2: list features actually present in the code path (Y/N/UNKNOWN with file:line citations)
audit_methodology.step_3: classify each baseline (naive / modern / advanced / oracle_only)
audit_methodology.step_4: judge fairness of selected strongest realistic baseline
audit_methodology.step_5: compute benchmark trust scores (1-10) for baseline strength / production similarity / headline credibility
audit_methodology.step_6: determine outreach claims the benchmark can honestly support

feature_definitions.dynamic_batching: policy/scheduler dynamically batches requests at admission
feature_definitions.continuous_batching: vLLM/TensorRT-LLM-style decode-side batching where new requests join the batch each iteration
feature_definitions.autoscaling: replica/capacity count varies over time in response to load
feature_definitions.queue_aware: decision uses queue depth / queue wait p95 as input
feature_definitions.latency_aware: decision uses latency / TTFT / SLA budget as input
feature_definitions.timeout_aware: decision uses timeout-rate signal as input
feature_definitions.cache_aware: decision uses KV/prefix cache reuse as input (or applies a prefill-savings model)
feature_definitions.affinity_aware: decision routes to a replica that already has the model/adapter resident
feature_definitions.placement_aware: decision picks WHICH node/GPU/topology to use, not just how many
feature_definitions.migration_aware: decision considers churn / migration cost / cold-cache penalty
feature_definitions.energy_aware: decision uses energy price signal as input
feature_definitions.carbon_aware: decision uses gCO2/kWh signal as input
feature_definitions.deadline_aware: decision uses job deadline / runtime feasibility window
feature_definitions.frontier_based: decision is a single static safe-utilization frontier operating point
feature_definitions.dynamic_frontier_based: decision uses a telemetry-driven adaptive frontier estimator

baseline_classes.naive: FIFO, static peak/mean overprovisioning, round-robin, no signal-driven adaptation
baseline_classes.modern: reactive autoscaling (HPA-style), queue-target or rho-target sizing, basic safety reverts. Comparable to what a competent operator using Kubernetes HPA / vLLM autoscaler would build
baseline_classes.advanced: anticipatory/EWMA sizing, multi-feature decisions (queue + cache + price + deadline + placement), price-aware heterogeneous routing, frontier-aware operating point selection
baseline_classes.oracle_only: future-leaking, clairvoyant, realized-optimal — analysis only, never deployable

baselines.name: fifo
baselines.impl_summary: policy=='fifo' sets fixed=True; uses _global_fixed_replicas(target_rho=0.70) → single replica count for the trace mean load

features.dynamic_batching: N — static replica count; serving physics has shared batching_efficiency but the POLICY does not size for it (aurelius/simulation/cluster/serving.py:114 batching_efficiency, applied identically to every policy)
features.continuous_batching: N — same shared serving-physics model, not a policy lever
features.latency_aware: N — no SLA/latency signal in the sizing path
features.placement_aware: N — no node/GPU selection (single homogeneous pool)
features.utilization_aware: Y — sizes for target_rho=0.70 on the MEAN load (aurelius/traces/backtest.py:319). This is static mean-sizing, not dynamic rho-tracking

baselines.rationale: Static replica count sized once for trace mean. The only 'modern' aspect is that it shares the simulator's continuous-batching physics (which every policy gets for free).

baselines.name: sla_aware
baselines.impl_summary: reactive autoscaler with one-tick lag at target_rho=0.50. Uses PREVIOUS tick's arrival_rate_rps to size replicas to keep utilization ≤ 0.50

features.dynamic_batching: N (policy does not size for batching)
features.latency_aware: PARTIAL — rho=0.50 is a CONSERVATIVE target chosen for SLA safety, but the policy does not measure latency directly; it just over-provisions a 2x safety margin

baselines.rationale: Reactive HPA-style autoscaler at a conservative utilization target. Comparable to a basic Kubernetes HPA on CPU/RPS. No queue/cache/affinity/prewarm. Has one-tick reaction lag.

baselines.name: queue_aware
baselines.impl_summary: scales replicas until predicted queue_wait_p95_ms ≤ 500ms (threshold). Uses PREVIOUS tick (one-tick lag).

features.autoscaling: Y — per-tick reactive sizing on queue signal
features.latency_aware: PARTIAL — queue wait is a component of latency, but the policy does not target SLA latency directly

baselines.rationale: Queue-target autoscaler — similar to HPA with custom queue-depth metric. Reactive. No cache, no affinity, no SLA-aware tail padding.

baselines.name: utilization_aware
baselines.impl_summary: per-tick _size_for_target(current_tick.arrival_rate, target_rho=UTIL_TARGET_RHO=0.85) — uses current-tick rate (so it implicitly has perfect knowledge of THIS tick's arrival; bordering on oracle for sizing but uses no future ticks)
baselines.rationale: Hot rho-target autoscaler. Higher goodput/$ on Azure 2024 but UNSAFE (timeout 12.10% > 10% gate). EXCLUDED from headline.

baselines.name: naive_overprovisioning
baselines.impl_summary: single static replica count sized for the trace's PEAK demand at SAFE_TARGET_RHO=0.50
baselines.rationale: Cost floor anti-pattern: cheap per GPU-hour idle, poor goodput/$. Anti-baseline only.

baselines.name: oracle_forecast_ANALYSIS_ONLY
baselines.impl_summary: per-tick perfect knowledge of THIS tick (no future leakage beyond current tick); sizes for FORECAST_TARGET_RHO
baselines.rationale: Analysis ceiling — never deployable. EXCLUDED from headline comparator per docs/RESULTS.md §3.

baselines.name: constraint_aware
baselines.impl_summary: anticipatory EWMA sizing (max of current + smoothed peak) at target_rho=0.65, with cache prefill savings (cache_aware=True) and hysteresis trim that probes downward while SLA stays safe (timeout_rate_pct ≤ 0). Optional frontier-controller opt-in lifts rho when shadow safety gates pass.

features.dynamic_batching: N (policy does not size for batching directly; relies on shared serving physics)
features.queue_aware: PARTIAL — implicit via Erlang-C wait inside evaluate_tick that constraint_trim consumes
features.affinity_aware: PARTIAL — BurstGPT has cache_affinity_key as a model-level proxy; not a real KV-aware affinity router
features.placement_aware: N (LLM serving backtest has a single homogeneous pool)
features.migration_aware: Y — hysteresis: avoids 1-replica churn when SLA-safe (aurelius/traces/backtest.py:447-451)
features.utilization_aware: Y — explicit target_rho=0.65; optionally lifted by frontier controller

baselines.rationale: Multi-feature autoscaler with anticipatory sizing + cache + SLA-aware trim + churn damping; opt-in frontier-based operating point selection.

benchmarks.alternative_baseline_if_any: queue_aware (also SAFE; numerically worse on this trace — 2.49M vs 2.03M goodput/$); utilization_aware (UNSAFE at 12.10% timeout — disqualified)
fairness_notes: sla_aware IS the strongest SAFE non-oracle baseline implemented in the harness — fair against what is implemented

BUT a real Azure / OpenAI / Anthropic stack would use vLLM/TensorRT-LLM/dynamo with KV-aware admission control, prefix caching, speculative decoding, and possibly custom autoscalers — features NONE of these baselines model directly. The simulator's serving physics gives every policy continuous batching for free, but does not model KV-aware admission as a policy lever.

constraint_aware's +25.75% over sla_aware is therefore +25.75% vs 'a reactive HPA autoscaler' — NOT vs 'a hyperscaler production inference stack'. The attribution doc shows the win is utilization-target / cost-efficiency, NOT a forecasting or scheduling effect, which is consistent with this being autoscaler-vs-autoscaler not stack-vs-stack.

baselines.name: sla_aware
baselines.notes: same _run_policy — reactive lag at target_rho=0.50; on BurstGPT exhibits worse p99/timeout, so cache_affinity_baseline is the chosen headline

baselines.name: cache_affinity_baseline
baselines.impl_summary: STATIC sizing at target_rho=0.70 (same as fifo) BUT with cache_aware=True so it gets MAX_PREFILL_SAVINGS * reuse_fraction prefill savings

features.affinity_aware: PARTIAL — uses the BurstGPT cache_affinity_key (model-level proxy; not real KV affinity)

baselines.rationale: Static-sizing baseline WITH a cache-savings proxy. Stronger than fifo because it benefits from the trace's reuse_fraction. Realistic for a workload where prefix cache is the dominant lever.

baselines.name: constraint_aware
baselines.notes: same implementation; on BurstGPT margin vs cache_affinity_baseline is +1.77%, vs sla_aware is +26.35%

benchmarks.alternative_baseline_if_any: sla_aware (larger margin but is itself unsafe-tail on this trace per the safety_evidence)
fairness_notes: cache_affinity_baseline is the strongest SAFE non-CA option on BurstGPT given the trace's cache_affinity_key proxy — fair pick.
Margin shrinks from +26% (vs sla_aware) to +1.77% (vs cache_affinity_baseline) — honest. The Aurelius rollup reports both.

Real production stacks (vLLM with prefix caching) would have a stronger cache-aware lever than the trace's model-level proxy; the +1.77% is therefore an upper bound, not a guarantee, of what would survive against a real KV-cache-aware serving stack.

baselines.name: fifo
baselines.notes: BEATS constraint_aware on this trace (mild burst, static cheapest)

baselines.name: sla_aware
baselines.notes: reactive lag at rho=0.50; constraint_aware +19.86% vs this

benchmarks.alternative_baseline_if_any: fifo (static do-nothing) BEATS constraint_aware on this trace at +7.5% — but fifo is the sanity baseline, not a realistic operator baseline; rollup labels this honestly as 'LOSS_vs_static_fifo'
fairness_notes: sla_aware is the strongest IMPLEMENTED non-static baseline on Azure 2023 — fair.
The trace has no cache/session/latency signal so cache_affinity_baseline is not applicable.
Azure 2023 conv is a mild-burst trace where STATIC sizing wins on goodput/$. The rollup is honest that fifo beats constraint_aware; the comparison vs sla_aware is the realistic-operator comparison.

baselines.name: fifo
baselines.impl_summary: static sizing at PEAK demand for TARGET_RHO_SLA; affinity=False

features.affinity_aware: N — affinity is set to (policy=='constraint_aware') only (aurelius/traces/genai_backtest.py:194)

baselines.name: sla_aware
baselines.impl_summary: reactive lag (uses prev_tick) + _size_for_sla loop that increases replicas until timeout_rate ≤ 0; affinity=False

features.latency_aware: Y (sizes until timeout ≤ 0 via Erlang-C/serving physics)
features.affinity_aware: N — CRITICAL GAP for GenAI: no model-affinity routing while there are 87 distinct base models

baselines.rationale: SLA-target reactive autoscaler — but on GenAI it lacks model-affinity, which the ablation shows is ~62% of the goodput/$ gain.

baselines.name: utilization_aware
baselines.notes: per-tick at TARGET_RHO_UTIL=0.85, affinity=False — but goodput/$ 6.83 below CA 9.84

baselines.name: constraint_aware
baselines.impl_summary: anticipatory (EWMA-peak) + _size_for_sla + AFFINITY=TRUE (cold-start amortised across requests for the same model)

features.affinity_aware: Y — affinity=True bypasses cold-start for warm-resident model (aurelius/traces/genai_backtest.py:194, 232-237)

benchmarks.alternative_baseline_if_any: utilization_aware (gpd 6.83 vs CA 9.84 → +44% margin, more honest comparison since both lack affinity); or, more accurately, the missing baseline 'sla_aware + model-affinity' (NOT implemented in this harness)
fairness_notes: CRITICAL FAIRNESS GAP: only constraint_aware has affinity=True in this harness. None of the baselines (fifo/sla_aware/queue_aware/utilization_aware) model model-affinity routing.
The ablation explicitly attributes ~62% of CA's +89.46% gain to affinity/prewarm (aurelius/traces/genai_ablation.py shapley_attribution: affinity_share_pct=62.1, sizing_share_pct=37.9).

A REAL stable-diffusion serving stack (Replicate, RunPod, AWS Inferentia, Hugging Face Inference Endpoints, in-house Anthropic/OpenAI image-gen stacks) ABSOLUTELY has model-affinity routing for cold-start mitigation — it is table-stakes when you have 87 distinct base models. The +89.46% headline is therefore +89.46% vs an UNDER-BASELINED comparator, not against a realistic operator.
If a 'sla_aware_with_affinity' baseline were implemented, the expected margin would collapse from +89% to approximately +34% (the sizing_share Shapley term, 37.9% of 89.46%).

Recommendation: outreach using GenAI numbers MUST disclose this gap, or restrict claims to the +44% utilization_aware comparison (also implemented; also lacks affinity, but at least it is a hot rho-target autoscaler), or build an explicit 'sla_aware_with_affinity' baseline before re-headlining.

baselines.name: fifo
baselines.impl_summary: round-robin among fitting nodes (no consolidation)

baselines.name: first_fit
baselines.impl_summary: lowest-index node that fits

baselines.name: best_fit
baselines.impl_summary: tightest remaining GPU capacity that fits, preferring active nodes (consolidation)

baselines.name: first_fit_decreasing
baselines.impl_summary: decreasing GPU demand (big jobs first) then first_fit

baselines.name: greedy_packing
baselines.impl_summary: FFD job order + best_fit node selection

baselines.name: constraint_aware
baselines.impl_summary: consolidate (prefer active) + cheapest adequate GPU TYPE (heterogeneous price-aware) + tightest fit + reserve big nodes for big jobs

benchmarks.alternative_baseline_if_any: greedy_packing (FFD + best_fit) — numerically identical to best_fit on this trace
fairness_notes: best_fit is the standard reference for GPU packing (Yarn/Kubernetes scheduler default in many configs).
constraint_aware adds heterogeneous price-aware GPU-type selection on top of best_fit consolidation, which IS what a sophisticated multi-cloud operator (CoreWeave, Lambda, RunPod) would do.
+6.0% margin is small but real and fair — packing baselines are already strong; the lever here is heterogeneous-fleet price awareness.

baselines.name: fifo
baselines.impl_summary: strict head-of-line; no backfill

baselines.name: first_fit / best_fit / FFD / greedy_packing / topology_aware / utilization_aware
baselines.impl_summary: backfill (scan ready in submit order, place any that fit) + node selection variant per policy

baselines.name: constraint_aware
baselines.impl_summary: matches best_fit/topology_aware/greedy_packing exactly on Philly (no GPU price signal; CA's heterogeneous-pricing lever is inactive)

fairness_notes: best_fit + backfill is comparable to YARN / Slurm / Kubernetes default schedulers — fair comparator.
Result is a TIE at fixture-scale (n=33 jobs). The full ~1 GB Philly LFS tarball would give a stronger statistical signal but does not change the qualitative finding.
Honest interpretation: constraint_aware does NOT beat best_fit on Philly because Philly publishes no GPU price (CA's main lever is inactive). The TIE is genuinely a tie, not a loss.

baselines.name: first_fit / best_fit / FFD / greedy_packing / topology_aware / utilization_aware
baselines.notes: all numerically identical on this trace (no GPU price, no topology penalty)

baselines.name: constraint_aware
baselines.notes: ties all of the above exactly

benchmarks.alternative_baseline_if_any: any of {first_fit_decreasing, topology_aware, greedy_packing, utilization_aware} — all identical
fairness_notes: Strong packing baselines compared honestly — TIE outcome (CA matches them exactly).
FIFO is UNSAFE here, so CA's +16% vs FIFO is also a SAFETY win; that is honestly framed in the rollup.

baselines.name: fifo
baselines.impl_summary: ASAP at earliest_start in default region

baselines.name: current_price_only
baselines.impl_summary: cheapest region at earliest_start (no temporal shift, no forecasting)

baselines.name: greedy_energy
baselines.impl_summary: cheapest (region, hour) in feasible window on DA price; ignores migration/basis/SLA

features.deadline_aware: PARTIAL (uses deadline-runtime as window upper bound)
features.migration_aware: N (no warmup awareness — that is why it misses 119 deadlines)

baselines.name: robust_energy_standalone
baselines.impl_summary: The Aurelius energy engine itself: greedy search over (time × region × power) with carbon, risk, GPU-health, queue, and migration penalties

features.migration_aware: PARTIAL (migration network cost in objective; but does not budget the warmup-hours cost of cross-region moves for non-home destinations)

baselines.notes: UNSAFE on this scoring (143 deadline misses) — the engine ignores warmup which the deadline scoring penalises

baselines.name: sla_aware
baselines.impl_summary: energy engine + reverts latency-pinned + deadline-unsafe moves to home (no destination/KPI gates)
baselines.notes: UNSAFE on this scoring (143 deadline misses)

baselines.name: constraint_aware_with_energy_adapter
baselines.impl_summary: energy engine ranked candidates → constraint-aware gates (eligibility / destination / SLA / KPI) → next-best safe fallback. Accepts the first SLA-safe AND KPI-positive candidate from {engine_optimized, current_price_only, home}

benchmarks.alternative_baseline_if_any: robust_energy_standalone (UNSAFE — disqualified by 143 deadline misses); the energy engine itself is the closest comparable advanced baseline but the harness disqualifies it
fairness_notes: Among SAFE non-CA policies, current_price_only is the strongest realistic baseline — fair.

Note that robust_energy_standalone is Aurelius's OWN energy engine; the comparison vs current_price_only is therefore 'wrapped Aurelius vs price-lookup baseline', and the +11% headline is the value the wrapper adds over a naive price-lookup operator.

A real sophisticated operator using a commercial DR / arbitrage product (e.g., Grid+, Voltus, Enchanted Rock) would have something between current_price_only and robust_energy_standalone — closer to greedy_energy with safety reverts. The +11% is therefore a fair representation of incremental value the wrapper adds over a competent-but-not-sophisticated operator.

baselines.name: frontier_controller_v1
baselines.impl_summary: estimates the Pareto frontier of (rho, predicted goodput/$, safety) and selects the highest SLA-safe goodput/$ point with safety vetoes (timeout/queue/latency/telemetry gates)
baselines.rationale: Self-comparison: CA-default vs CA+frontier-controller. Not an external baseline.

fairness_notes: This is a SELF-AUDIT comparing CA-default vs CA+frontier-controller — not an external benchmark. The +12.98% on Azure 2024 is CA-on-CA improvement, not CA-vs-baseline alpha.
When folded into outreach numbers, this should be reported as a SEPARATE 'controller adds X% on top of CA' figure, never combined with the headline CA-vs-sla_aware margin.

baselines.name: constraint_aware_static (rho=0.65)
baselines.notes: default constraint_aware path in aurelius/traces/backtest.py:393-404 — full feature evidence in azure_llm_2024_week row above

baselines.name: dynamic_frontier_estimator
baselines.notes: telemetry-replay window-based rho selection; retains 73% of oracle alpha

fairness_notes: Pure calibration audit; the 91.07% oracle-alpha-capture is a quality metric of the dynamic estimator, NOT a goodput/$ headline.
EXCLUDED from headline rollup.

benchmarks.selected_strongest_realistic_baseline_in: best safe candidate in grid (rho=0.95, c=1, slack=0.5h)
fairness_notes: This is a frontier recommendation, not a baseline comparison. No fifo/sla_aware/etc baseline is run.
EXCLUDED from headline rollup (the original rollup correctly does not include eval/batch frontiers in the 8 safe-eligible benchmarks).
Same as eval_workload_frontier — recommendation-only, not a baseline comparison.
Not in the headline rollup.

benchmark_trust_scores._doc: 1-10 each. baseline_strength_score: how strong is the strongest realistic baseline relative to a real production stack. production_similarity_score: how close the simulated environment is to a real customer environment. headline_credibility_score: composite (baseline_strength × honest_caveat_disclosure × applicable_workload_breadth).

azure_llm_2024_week.notes: sla_aware is modern-but-basic; real Azure/OpenAI stacks have KV-aware admission + prefix caching not modelled here. Trace is real and large (44.1M rows, 9 days).

burstgpt_v1.notes: cache_affinity_baseline is the right comparator given the trace's signal. Trace has only model-level cache proxy (not real KV).

azure_llm_2023_conv.notes: Static FIFO beats CA on this mild-burst trace — honest. Margin is real vs sla_aware.

alibaba_genai_2026.notes: CRITICAL: only constraint_aware has affinity=True. None of the baselines model model-affinity routing despite 87 distinct base models. The +89% headline is under-baselined; expected fair margin if a sla_aware+affinity baseline existed: ~+34% (sizing Shapley term only).

alibaba_gpu_v2023.notes: best_fit / FFD / greedy_packing are standard production scheduler baselines (YARN/K8s). +6% margin is small but fair.

philly_training.notes: best_fit + backfill is comparable to Slurm. But fixture-scale (n=33) is a sample-size issue. TIE outcome is honest.

mit_supercloud_bounded.notes: Standard packing baselines all numerically identical; TIE is honest. Real 10,000-job Slurm sample.

canonical_energy_backtest.notes: current_price_only is a fair safe baseline. Synthetic workload mix limits production similarity.

azure_llm_2024_week.can_support_vs_modern_scheduler_claims: YES — vs sla_aware/queue_aware reactive HPA-style baselines, +26% / +3%
azure_llm_2024_week.can_support_vs_hyperscaler_style_schedul: NO — none of the baselines model KV-aware admission, prefix caching, custom autoscalers, or speculative decoding. Disclose this gap.
azure_llm_2024_week.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: YES WITH CAVEATS — pair with the attribution disclosure: 'win is utilization-target / cost-efficiency, not forecasting accuracy'. Frame as 'better than reactive autoscaling at parity SLA safety', not as 'better than a sophisticated inference stack'.

burstgpt_v1.can_support_vs_fifo_claims: MARGINAL — fifo beats CA by 2% on goodput/$ in some configs; safety story holds
burstgpt_v1.can_support_vs_hyperscaler_style_schedul: NO — real KV cache, real prefix caching, real session routing absent
burstgpt_v1.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: MARGINAL — the +1.77% vs cache_affinity is small; better to lead with the LLM-serving median, not BurstGPT alone

azure_llm_2023_conv.can_support_vs_fifo_claims: NO — fifo BEATS CA on this trace; disclose
azure_llm_2023_conv.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: WEAK — only viable as 'one of three LLM traces; the median is the headline'

alibaba_genai_2026.can_support_vs_modern_scheduler_claims: PARTIALLY — none of the baselines have affinity, which is the dominant lever; honest comparator is utilization_aware where CA still wins +44%
alibaba_genai_2026.can_support_vs_hyperscaler_style_schedul: NO — a real SD serving stack has model-affinity routing; this benchmark UNDER-BASELINES the comparison
alibaba_genai_2026.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: USE WITH STRONG DISCLOSURE: 'Aurelius adds model-affinity routing where the trace's stable-diffusion serving has 87 distinct base models — a feature any real production stack would also have.' Quote +44% vs utilization_aware (the fairest implemented comparator), not +89% vs sla_aware (under-baselined).

alibaba_gpu_v2023.can_support_vs_hyperscaler_style_schedul: PARTIALLY — best_fit is what most real schedulers do; a hyperscaler with heterogeneous-fleet-aware bin-packing (e.g., Borg) would be closer to CA
alibaba_gpu_v2023.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: YES — +6% economic alpha + heterogeneous-fleet price awareness story

philly_training.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: Frame as 'matches strongest packing baseline — already on safe frontier for this workload class'. DO NOT make CA-vs-modern-scheduler claims here.

mit_supercloud_bounded.can_support_vs_modern_scheduler_claims: NO — TIES every safe packing baseline
mit_supercloud_bounded.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: Frame as 'safe-frontier-tied + safety win vs naive FIFO'.

canonical_energy_backtest.can_support_vs_modern_scheduler_claims: YES — +11% vs current_price_only at 0 deadline misses
canonical_energy_backtest.can_support_vs_hyperscaler_style_schedul: PARTIALLY — commercial DR/arbitrage products are between current_price_only and the energy engine; the +11% is incremental over a competent-but-not-sophisticated operator
canonical_energy_backtest.can_support_enterprise_outreach_claims: YES — SAFETY_WIN with alpha is a compelling enterprise story

final_question_answer.question: If a sophisticated Azure/OpenAI/Anthropic-style inference stack already uses dynamic batching, queue-aware scheduling, and autoscaling, how much of Aurelius' reported improvement remains after comparing against the strongest implemented realistic baseline?
final_question_answer.short_answer: Roughly half to two-thirds of the headline survives — but a hyperscaler-equivalent baseline is NOT implemented in any current Aurelius benchmark. The honest range is +10% to +25% on LLM-serving traces vs the strongest implemented modern baseline (sla_aware/queue_aware/cache_affinity_baseline), with NO benchmark that simulates a real KV-aware + prefix-caching + model-affinity production stack.

what_already_holds: Continuous batching is in the shared serving physics (aurelius/simulation/cluster/serving.py:114), so every policy in the LLM-serving backtests gets it for free. The Aurelius vs sla_aware comparisons are NOT 'with continuous batching vs without' — they are autoscaler-vs-autoscaler.

Queue-aware scheduling exists as queue_aware baseline (aurelius/traces/backtest.py:387-392) and as an input to constraint_aware's _constraint_trim. Aurelius wins +3% vs queue_aware on Azure 2024 — that small margin survives against a queue-aware HPA-style baseline.

Autoscaling exists as sla_aware (rho=0.50), queue_aware (queue-target), utilization_aware (rho=0.85) — three implemented modern reactive baselines. Aurelius's +26% vs sla_aware on Azure 2024 is the relevant headline; +3% vs queue_aware is the conservative floor.
what_does_NOT_hold_against_a_real_hypers: KV-aware admission control: no baseline implements per-request KV-cache-pressure-aware admission. Production stacks (vLLM PagedAttention, TensorRT-LLM, Anthropic / OpenAI / DeepMind inference) do.

Prefix caching as a scheduler input: cache_affinity_baseline uses a STATIC PRIOR (MAX_PREFILL_SAVINGS=0.25 × reuse_fraction), not a real per-request prefix-cache router. vLLM / SGLang / Mooncake have real prefix-cache-aware admission.

Model-affinity routing: ONLY constraint_aware on GenAI has affinity=True. Real multi-model serving (Replicate, RunPod, Hugging Face Endpoints, Anthropic batch model multiplexing) has model-affinity routing as a primary feature. On GenAI this is the +62% (~Shapley) portion of CA's gain.

Speculative decoding, prompt-aware batching, chunked prefill, disaggregated prefill/decode (DistServe, Sarathi-Serve, Mooncake): NOT modelled. Aurelius's serving physics is a single shared continuous-batching model.
Custom autoscalers calibrated to per-tenant SLOs: a hyperscaler operator would have profile-specific scaling policies; the implemented sla_aware/queue_aware are generic.

azure_llm_2024_week.estimation_rationale: +2.6% vs queue_aware sets a floor; +5-15% accounts for the static-frontier-controller's +12.98% being available to CA via opt-in (which a hyperscaler stack would also use)

burstgpt_v1.estimation_rationale: cache_affinity_baseline already proxies prefix caching; a real vLLM prefix-cache-aware stack would erase most of the +1.77%

azure_llm_2023_conv.estimation_rationale: Static fifo already beats CA on this trace; a sophisticated stack with adaptive static-vs-reactive switching would likely match or beat CA

alibaba_genai_2026.estimation_rationale: Ablation Shapley: 62% from affinity (a hyperscaler stack would have this), 38% from sizing. +20-40% would be the sizing-only residual against a baseline that also had affinity routing

alibaba_gpu_v2023.estimation_rationale: best_fit already standard; Google Borg-style heterogeneous-fleet bin-packing would erase most of the +6%. CoreWeave/Lambda-style multi-cloud price arbitrage would NOT erase it (CA's price-aware lever survives)

philly_training_and_mit_supercloud.estimation_rationale: Already TIES standard packing baselines. Slurm-equivalent scheduler would also tie.

canonical_energy_backtest.estimation_rationale: A sophisticated commercial DR/arbitrage product would have something closer to greedy_energy + safety reverts; +3-11% residual depending on how much warmup-awareness the commercial product has

long_answer.honest_summary_for_outreach: Of the headline median +9% across 8 benchmarks (rollup): vs a realistic hyperscaler-equivalent baseline (NOT IMPLEMENTED, estimated directionally), the conservative estimated residual is roughly +3-15% on LLM-serving traces, +0-6% on GPU packing, +0% (tie) on training, +3-11% on energy. The +89% GenAI headline is most affected (collapses to ~+20-40% if a baseline had model-affinity routing).
what_to_build_next_to_close_the_gap: Implement a vLLM-style baseline with PagedAttention KV-aware admission + real prefix-cache routing (would tighten BurstGPT and Azure baselines).
Implement a model-affinity baseline for GenAI (would tighten the +89% headline to ~+34%).
Implement a Borg-style heterogeneous-fleet bin-packing baseline (would tighten the +6% Alibaba GPU headline).
Run on a customer-telemetry shadow-pilot (the docs/RESULTS.md §8 production-claim gate).
